body_txt: While white-collar jobs may be coveted in real life, they are not so great in fiction—office work is soul-crushingly boring, or just soul-crushingly empty in general. It has "stressful" and "unfulfilling" written all over it. The work you are required to do is often meaningless, repetitive and annoying. In customer service, you have to deal with disgruntled customers. Your colleagues are either a real pain, like the Weasel Co-Worker or the Professional Slacker , who you always have to cover for, making your life even worse, or if you're lucky, they may be your companions in the week-long suffering. Your boss will be a demanding, mean manager , an incompetent one , or both. Wisecracking bosses who are too jovial and too friendly are not popular with the team either because they'll still lay you off even if you've been nothing but loyal to the company. While Benevolent Bosses exist in real life, they are rare in fiction about office jobs. Senior management might be outright tyrannical. You are very likely to be yelled at. To top it off, you are all cogs in a huge bureaucracy filled with complex, Byzantine rules and procedures. At least the Burger Fool actually does something useful. The person who majored in A Degree in Useless often ends up in one of these. They might have envisioned living a life of the mind in college, but their reality after graduating is a life of paperwork. Some gentler souls are on the brink of being crushed by the corporate dog-eat-dog world. If you get lucky and get promoted, expect to deal with incompetent employees who seem to act stupid on purpose. And if you vent about this to friends or family members outside the company, you'll often receive No Sympathy , because you have what's allegedly a "good" job (i.e., one that can be done indoors, involves little or no manual labor, and often pays better than "blue-collar" jobs do, and provides dental and health benefits ). And let's not even get started on the hellstorm of health problems that result from the working conditions, from repetitive strain injuries to depression. In fiction, it's often presented as a source of comedy in Work Coms . Alternatively, it can appear for one episode only as a New Job Episode . Or it's a good starting point for a Character Development , when the character decides they can't take it anymore and realizes it's time to quit, ideally to Pursue the Dream Job or possibly just to accept Happiness in Minimum Wage . If the character starts rebelling, it may lead to a glorious " Take This Job and Shove It " situation. The white-collar equivalent of the Soul-Sucking Retail Job , though the Soul-Crushing Desk Job has a slightly better reputation in fiction—you have to have at least some skills and a decent education, and the wages might be better, though still nothing to write home about. This is often paired with the drab uniformity of the Standard Office Setting , although the Wacky Startup Workplace might induce this as well. Desk Jockey and Post-Injury Desk Job are military/law enforcement sister tropes. This trope is born from the inherent bias of writers being … well, writers . To someone who thrives on creativity, the thought of spending eight hours a day in a cubicle typing up reports is pure hell, even though plenty of people prefer a work environment with stability and routine, and there's no telling what their hobbies are like after hours . But per Rule of Drama , "Alice the actuary enjoys her job" doesn't make for compelling fiction, and white-collar discontent has been studied academically. A good example would be anarchist economic anthropologist David Graeber's concept of "bullshit jobs." See also Abusive Workplace and Paperwork Punishment .
